notes.anchor: the seventeenth day before the Kalends of May
notes.note: A Roman date formula (ante diem xvi Kal. Maias), counted inclusively backward from the Kalends (first) of May; this is 16 April. The translation renders the inclusive count literally as "the seventeenth day."

C. Scribonius Curio the younger (the future tribune of 50 BC), then a brash young man whom Cicero courts as an opponent of the coalition. "He hates haughty kings" plays on his contempt for the dynasts dominating the state.

notes.anchor: you may suppose I am Saufeius
notes.note: L. Saufeius, an Epicurean friend of Atticus famous for philosophical detachment and inactivity. Cicero jokes that in retiring to write history he is as idle as Saufeius --- then caps it: "Nothing is lazier than I."
The Parilia (or Palilia), the rustic festival of Pales on 21 April, also celebrated as the traditional birthday of Rome. The bracketed date is editorial, supplied for the reader.

Cicero's playful figure (cratera illum delicatum) for Antium, the seaside resort whose games he is being advised to skip; literally "that elegant mixing-bowl," i.e. an enticing draught of luxury to be set aside this season.
The Kalends were the first day of the Roman month; here 1 May. Romans named the three fixed points of each month --- Kalends, Nones, Ides --- and counted other days backward from them.

notes.anchor: on the fifth day before the Nones of May
notes.note: The Nones fell on the 7th in May (and in March, July, October), on the 5th in other months; counted inclusively, the fifth day before is 3 May.
